preamble: The transformation from a voiced aspirated bilabial plosive (bʱ) to a voiceless labiodental fricative (f) is a regular historical phonetic shift, primarily driven by lenition processes. This involves the weakening of the consonant through spirantization (a stop becoming a fricative) and devoicing. A notable instance of this shift is observed in the development of Proto-Indo-European voiced aspirated stops in the Italic branch of the Indo-European language family.
phoneticEffects: Lenition, Spirantization, Devoicing, Place Assimilation (bilabial to labiodental)

examples.note: The Proto-Indo-European voiced aspirated bilabial stop (*bʰ), which is the ancestor of bʱ, became the voiceless labiodental fricative (f) in word-initial position in Latin. For example, PIE *bʰer- 'to bear' became Latin ferō 'I bear'.

sources: What is the difference between a breathy-voiced consonant ( ʱ) and an aspirated consonant ( ʰ), and which of the two are the ones used in the Indo-Aryan languages? - Quora
